query: saliva based diagnostic testing infectious disease

items.title: Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.
items.summary: The study investigated whether a low-cost, noninvasive electronic nose could reliably detect SARS-CoV-2 as an alternative to conventional diagnostic methods that are slow, uncomfortable, or inaccurate in early infection. The device used an array of commercially available metal-oxide gas sensors to capture and analyze volatile organic compounds in both saliva and exhaled breath, which shift in composition in the presence of the virus. Several machine learning algorithms were applied to classify infected versus non-infected samples, and their discriminatory performance was compared. The approach achieved accuracy ranging from the mid-seventies to the upper eighties across both sample types, with the best performance obtained using a nearest-neighbor classifier on exhaled breath. The authors conclude that the electronic nose demonstrates feasible proof-of-concept performance for point-of-care SARS-CoV-2 screening in a real-world hospital setting.
items.venue: The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Fialkovitz G, Sousa PL, Hidifira AM et al.. Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.. The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.
items.summary: This study investigated the fungal microbiome (mycobiome) in patients with common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), a condition previously linked to bacterial dysbiosis but not well characterized in terms of its fungal communities. Using ITS1 amplicon sequencing across saliva, sputum, and stool samples from adults with CVID and healthy controls, the researchers profiled fungal communities and applied diversity analyses, differential abundance testing, and machine learning approaches. They found that CVID patients exhibited significantly altered mycobiome composition and reduced fungal richness and evenness compared to controls, most notably in oral and respiratory samples, with a consistent enrichment of opportunistic yeasts—particularly *Candida albicans*—across all three body sites alongside depletion of environmental and commensal taxa. Importantly, this fungal dysbiosis did not clearly distinguish between CVID patients with immune dysregulation and those with infectious-only manifestations, suggesting the mycobiome shift is a shared feature of the disease. Machine learning models built on fungal profiles demonstrated strong discriminatory power for identifying CVID from controls, supporting the potential utility of fungal signatures as non-invasive diagnostic biomarkers and implicating *C. albicans* as a possible pathobiont in this immunodeficiency.
items.citation: Cabanero-Navalon MD, Carda-Diéguez M, Mira A et al.. Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.. Frontiers in immunology. 2026

items.title: Extraoral Detection of Biomarkers and Pathogens in Saliva: Comprehensive, Panoramic Review.
items.summary: This paper reviewed methods for detecting disease biomarkers and pathogens in human saliva through extraoral analysis, motivated by saliva's complex composition and the practical advantages of non-invasive sample collection. The authors conducted a comprehensive survey of analytical and clinical studies, compiling detection limits from analytical papers alongside sensitivity, specificity, and area-under-the-curve values from clinical studies to enable quantitative comparisons across techniques including mass spectrometry, PCR, ELISA, electrochemical platforms, and optical methods such as fluorescence, surface plasmon resonance, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering. Using cortisol as a case study for analytical sensitivity comparisons, electrochemical platforms—particularly voltammetric approaches—achieved the lowest detection limits, outperforming immunoassay and mass spectrometry methods. In clinical performance, ELISA and direct-SERS demonstrated the highest balanced accuracy and the strongest AUC values, with mass spectrometry and PCR performing comparably behind them. The review concludes that while electrochemical methods excel in analytical sensitivity and direct-SERS shows strong clinical promise, broader adoption of SERS requires addressing standardization challenges before it can be widely implemented in clinical diagnostics.
items.citation: Dyussupova A, Ilyas A, Boranova A et al.. Extraoral Detection of Biomarkers and Pathogens in Saliva: Comprehensive, Panoramic Review.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies
items.summary: This review examines the molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying inflammatory bone loss in periodontitis, a chronic disease driven by dysbiotic oral biofilms that destroy the periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone. The authors synthesize current knowledge on osteoclastogenesis and the key transcriptional regulators and signaling pathways that govern osteoclast differentiation and activation, including MAPK, JAK/STAT, NF-κB, and PI3K/Akt pathways. The review highlights how dysregulation of these pathways orchestrates chronic inflammation, immune dysregulation, and tissue destruction characteristic of periodontitis, drawing parallels to other osteolytic conditions such as osteoporosis and rheumatoid arthritis. Current and emerging therapeutic strategies are discussed, with particular focus on small molecules, biologics, and natural compounds capable of modulating these pathways. The authors conclude that while promising therapeutic targets have been identified, translating mechanistic insights into effective clinical interventions remains a significant challenge requiring continued research.
authors: Jeroen J. J. P. van den Beucken
items.citation: Rafael Scaf de Molon, Rolando Vernal, Gabriela Ezequiel Oliveira et al.. Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies. Bone Research. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review paper examines the role of the gut microbiome as a causal driver of colorectal cancer, moving beyond traditional genetic and environmental explanations to explore how microbial communities mechanistically contribute to cancer initiation, progression, and treatment response. The authors synthesize molecular, ecological, and translational evidence to explain how dysbiotic microbial communities reprogram immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine networks within the tumor microenvironment, with pathobiont enrichment and loss of protective commensals being central features. Specific bacterial toxins and metabolites, including colibactin, cytolethal distending toxin, and indolimines, are shown to leave defined mutational signatures in human cancer genomes, directly linking microbial exposures to carcinogenesis through mechanisms such as DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, and chronic inflammation. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which microbial consortia cooperate to drive barrier breakdown, immune evasion, and metabolic reprogramming. It concludes by highlighting emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies, including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation, positioning the microbiome as a tractable target for precision prevention and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication that carry lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids reflective of their parent cells' physiological and pathological states. The authors surveyed the major classes of extracellular vesicles, including exosomes, microvesicles, apoptotic bodies, and oncosomes, covering their biogenesis, isolation methods, molecular characterization, and omics-based profiling. Particular attention was given to the roles these vesicles play in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology, as well as the emerging therapeutic applications of mesenchymal stem cell-derived vesicles in regenerative medicine and the modulation of vesicle signaling through photobiomodulation. The review also addresses critical translational obstacles, including the need for standardization, scalability, and regulatory harmonization under frameworks such as those imposed by the FDA and EMA. The authors conclude that extracellular vesicles hold transformative potential as next-generation diagnostic and therapeutic tools within precision medicine, provided that key technical and regulatory barriers are resolved.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology—commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems—and their expanding role in laboratory medicine. The authors surveyed innovations in chip design and fabrication, including three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, alongside the integration of advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes in real time. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms that support self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support across applications including cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, and point-of-care testing. The review also highlights emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, illustrating the breadth of potential clinical impact. Current challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration are discussed alongside future directions for translating these technologies into routine clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration
items.summary: This review examines the current state of smart wearable and implantable biosensors designed for continuous, real-time monitoring of biophysical and biochemical signals in support of personalized and preventive healthcare. The paper surveys advances in flexible, stretchable, and biocompatible materials that enable long-term body integration, alongside multimodal and multi-analyte sensing approaches intended to improve measurement robustness. The integration of artificial intelligence for signal processing and predictive health insights is also discussed as a key enabling strategy. The review identifies persistent challenges including motion artifacts, energy autonomy, data privacy, and the clinical interpretability of sensor outputs. Overall, the work consolidates knowledge across materials science, device architecture, and AI-assisted sensing to map both progress and remaining obstacles in the field.
items.citation: Thirumalaisamy Suryaprabha, Chunghyeon Choi, Yanfang Wu et al.. Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration. npj Flexible Electronics. 2026

items.title: Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential
items.summary: This review paper examines the current state of electrochemical biosensors designed to detect protein biomarkers in blood for cancer diagnosis and prognosis. The authors critically survey general strategies for electrochemical detection of serum proteins alongside recent advances in liquid biopsy platforms targeting cancer-related proteins, noting that many approaches have achieved impressive analytical sensitivity. The review identifies major obstacles preventing these technologies from reaching clinical use, including interference from complex biological sample matrices, insufficient validation with real patient samples, challenges in accounting for variability between patients, and practical hurdles such as manufacturing scalability, cost, and long-term device stability. Particular emphasis is placed on the analytical and clinical validation steps necessary for successful translation, as robust non-invasive liquid biopsy tools for protein biomarkers remain scarce despite their potential to improve early cancer detection and treatment.
items.citation: Michael E. J. Lopez Mujica, Elena E. Ferapontova. Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards
items.summary: This paper investigates whether a unified large multimodal model capable of both visual understanding and image generation can improve itself autonomously without relying on human annotations, preference labels, or external reward models. The authors propose a self-evolving training framework built around three internal roles: a Proposer that generates visual questions, a Solver that answers and evaluates them, and a Generator that synthesizes images, with training driven entirely by self-derived consistency signals. To stabilize learning when sample-level consistency signals become unreliable, they introduce a token-level prediction uncertainty measure called Solver Token Entropy as a continuous difficulty signal, and they design a multi-scale internal evaluation scheme for image generation that couples question-answer fidelity with cycle-consistent captioning. The framework is architecture-agnostic, validated across diffusion-based, rectified-flow, and autoregressive model backbones using only each model's native interface. Across multiple understanding and generation benchmarks, the method consistently improves over the corresponding base models, demonstrating that unified multimodal models can self-improve both visual understanding and image generation without external supervision.
items.citation: Ritesh Thawkar, Shravan Venkatraman, Omkar Thawakar et al.. Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models
items.summary: This paper investigates a failure mode in self-evolving large multimodal models (LMMs) called visual under-conditioning, where models rely on language priors rather than actual image content during generation due to reward schemes that optimize answer agreement without ensuring attention to visual tokens. The authors propose VISE (Visual Invariance Self-Evolution), a purely unsupervised framework that addresses this problem through two complementary reward signals: a geometric invariance reward enforcing spatial consistency under known image transformations, and a semantic invariance reward that penalizes generation disconnected from visual evidence by requiring the model to recognize when predicted regions are perturbed. VISE operates within a single model without specialist roles, external reward models, or labeled data, training exclusively on raw unlabeled images. Evaluated across a broad set of benchmarks, the framework yields substantial improvements in image captioning and visual question answering performance, as well as meaningful reductions in object hallucination, and generalizes across multiple model families and scales.
items.citation: Shravan Venkatraman, Ritesh Thawkar, Omkar Thawakar et al.. Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks
items.summary: This paper investigates a limitation of standard softmax-based multihead attention in vision transformers, specifically that it can produce noisy attention patterns that dilute relevant features and reduce model performance. The authors propose Denoising Attention (DnA), a method that uses a positive query to identify relevant image features and a negative query to identify closely associated but irrelevant features, then projects these interactions into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles to improve discriminability. This subspace separation design is intended to suppress irrelevant feature contributions and sharpen attention. The approach is evaluated on image classification, video understanding, and video language models, yielding consistent improvements across all tasks compared to softmax attention baselines. Empirical analyses are provided to justify the dual-subspace design and validate the denoising effect of the proposed mechanism.
items.citation: Ron Campos, Subhajit Maity, Xin Li et al.. DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dirac fermions in non-Hermitian magnetic fields: Zero modes and index theorem
items.summary: This paper investigates the behavior of massless Dirac fermions coupled to non-Hermitian gauge fields arising from spatially modulated non-Hermitian parameters in a Lorentz-symmetric non-Hermitian Dirac theory. When such a system encloses a finite flux of non-Hermitian magnetic fields, the authors find that spatially localized zero-energy eigenmodes of purely right- or left-handed chirality emerge in the spectrum, analogous to the Atiyah-Singer index theorem in standard Hermitian systems. These zero modes are numerically confirmed through microscopic realizations on graphene's honeycomb lattice, connecting the theoretical framework to a concrete physical platform. The resulting non-Hermitian flat bands of chiral zero modes are proposed as a foundation for future phenomena including non-Hermitian magnetic catalysis, fractional topological phases, and chiral anomaly in non-Hermitian settings.
items.citation: Christopher A. Leong, Bitan Roy. Dirac fermions in non-Hermitian magnetic fields: Zero modes and index theorem. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs
items.summary: This paper investigates whether large language models can be improved through reinforcement learning on optimization tasks that lack ground-truth solutions, a setting where standard reward-based training approaches are inapplicable. The authors introduce RiVER, a framework that uses deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision and applies group-relative reinforcement learning to score-based heuristic contest problems. They identify and address two failure modes in this setting—scale dominance and frequency dominance—through calibrated reward shaping that emphasizes top-ranked solutions while maintaining bounded feedback for other valid outputs. Training exclusively on heuristic contest tasks without any ground-truth answers, RiVER substantially improves model performance not only on optimization benchmarks but also transfers to exact-solution coding benchmarks, whereas naive baselines using raw execution scores fail to generalize beyond the training task type. The results suggest that properly calibrated score-based optimization tasks can serve as effective training environments for developing general coding capabilities in the absence of ground-truth solutions.
items.citation: Yingyu Lin, Qiyue Gao, Nikki Lijing Kuang et al.. Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Hidden-ordered Dirac fermions
items.summary: This paper proposes a theoretical extension of the standard Lorentz-symmetric Dirac theory by adding a second masslike anticommuting Dirac operator to the Hamiltonian while preserving its Hermitian character. The resulting framework produces quasiparticle excitations, termed hidden-ordered Dirac fermions, that retain the linear energy-momentum dispersion relation in arbitrary spatial dimensions and are symmetry protected, exhibiting responses analogous to conventional Dirac systems but with a renormalized Fermi velocity reflecting the hidden ordering. A key finding is that this hidden ordering generally shifts quantum phase transitions toward insulating states at stronger coupling for dimensions greater than one, though whether the hidden order survives or vanishes near an itinerant quantum critical point depends on the algebraic relationship between the candidate insulating order parameter and the masslike Dirac operator. The author constructs explicit lattice models realizing these hidden-ordered massless Dirac fermions and identifies candidate numerical and experimental platforms for testing the theoretical predictions.
items.citation: Bitan Roy. Hidden-ordered Dirac fermions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Why Andes hantavirus is not the next SARS-CoV-2: contrasting viral shedding, transmissibility and genomic patterns.
items.venue: Euro surveillance : bulletin Europeen sur les maladies transmissibles = European communicable disease bulletin
items.citation: Bal A, Sauvage V, Bouscambert-Duchamp M et al.. Why Andes hantavirus is not the next SARS-CoV-2: contrasting viral shedding, transmissibility and genomic patterns.. Euro surveillance : bulletin Europeen sur les maladies transmissibles = European communicable disease bulletin. 2026

items.title: The Salivary Microbiota–Host Nexus: Unraveling Opportunities for Non-Invasive Monitoring of Health and Productivity in Farm Animals
items.venue: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
items.citation: The Salivary Microbiota–Host Nexus: Unraveling Opportunities for Non-Invasive Monitoring of Health and Productivity in Farm Animals. Animals : an open access journal from MDPI. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing beyond conventional laboratory settings. The authors surveyed the field by organizing their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they assessed approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods, evaluating their suitability for simplified and equipment-free deployment. Amplification strategies reviewed include both thermal cycling methods such as polymerase chain reaction and isothermal alternatives such as loop-mediated isothermal amplification and recombinase polymerase amplification, all adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review covers platforms ranging from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensors leveraging CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles, which together offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition at the point of care.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

query: group A streptococcus molecular diagnostic test

items.title: Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A, a rapid quantitative PCR assay originally designed for throat swabs, for detecting *Streptococcus pyogenes* from pediatric pleural fluids in the context of invasive Group A streptococcal pneumonia. The researchers compared Xpert results against a composite reference standard comprising bacterial culture and a laboratory-developed qPCR assay targeting a different gene, using a convenience sample of pleural fluid specimens from pediatric patients. Xpert achieved perfect agreement with the composite reference standard and with the laboratory-developed qPCR, detecting all positive specimens, whereas culture alone identified only a minority of those same positive cases. The findings demonstrate that Xpert is substantially more sensitive than culture for identifying *S. pyogenes* in pleural fluid, likely because many patients had received antibiotics before sample collection. The authors conclude that Xpert offers both improved diagnostic accuracy and faster turnaround time, supporting its utility for guiding clinical management and public health responses to invasive Group A streptococcal disease.
items.citation: Amaral G, Gadkar VJ, Almodahka A et al.. Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives
items.summary: Silver nanoparticles have attracted significant research interest as potential alternatives to conventional antibiotics, particularly given the global rise of drug-resistant pathogens. This review examines the antibacterial mechanisms of silver nanoparticles, which include the release of silver ions that disrupt bacterial cell membranes, generation of reactive oxygen species, inhibition of protein synthesis, and interference with DNA replication. The physicochemical properties of these nanoparticles, including their shape, size, and surface characteristics, are identified as key determinants of their efficacy and specificity against a broad range of bacteria including antibiotic-resistant strains. The review also surveys their practical applications across agricultural, biomedical, and environmental sectors, while acknowledging that inappropriate dosing or suboptimal administration can result in significant toxicity, representing a major challenge for their safe use. The authors conclude by discussing future perspectives for optimizing silver nanoparticle use in antibacterial contexts.
items.citation: Hasan Karataş, Furkan Eker, Emir Akdaşçi et al.. Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms through which the microbiota modulates oncogenic pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of the microbiota as both a promoter and suppressor of tumor growth, as well as its role in shaping responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota, including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation, are evaluated alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by calling for personalized approaches to exploit the anticancer potential of the microbiota and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical applications in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases
items.summary: This review paper examines organ cross-talk, the communication networks between organs that maintain physiological homeostasis and coordinate responses to environmental stimuli. The authors conducted a comprehensive synthesis of current research on inter-organ communication, with particular focus on nine key bilateral connections involving the brain, heart, and gut, including axes such as the brain-gut, heart-kidney, and gut-liver, while also cataloguing additional organ interactions in tabular form. The review systematically describes the molecular mechanisms and signaling pathways underlying these interactions in both healthy and disease states, drawing on cutting-edge observations and visualizing the research landscape through a Sankey diagram. Based on these mechanistic insights, the authors propose multiple therapeutic strategies with potential clinical application and discuss the methodologies and advanced technologies used to study organ connectivity. The paper ultimately advocates for network-driven, systems biology approaches combined with personalized treatment strategies as a framework for understanding and addressing complex diseases.
items.citation: Huiting Che, Yidan Gao, Yonghu Xu et al.. Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Gut Microbiota-Derived Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Mechanistic Insights into Gut Inflammation, Barrier Function, and Therapeutic Potential
items.summary: This review examines the role of gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids in the context of inflammatory bowel disease, encompassing both Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. The authors synthesize existing research on how SCFAs, particularly acetate, propionate, and butyrate, are produced through microbial fermentation of dietary fiber and exert wide-ranging effects on intestinal homeostasis, including strengthening epithelial barrier integrity, modulating local and systemic immune responses, and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine expression. Special emphasis is placed on butyrate's function as an energy source for colonocytes and its role in enhancing mucosal defense mechanisms. The review critically evaluates the therapeutic potential of these metabolites in IBD management, noting that clinical trials have produced variable and inconsistent results, likely attributable to differences in study design and patient characteristics. The authors conclude that while SCFAs hold significant promise as therapeutic agents, a deeper mechanistic understanding of their interactions with the intestinal barrier, immune system, and microbiota is needed to translate this potential into reliable clinical strategies.
items.citation: Roberta Ottria, Susan Mirmajidi, Pierangela Ciuffreda. Gut Microbiota-Derived Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Mechanistic Insights into Gut Inflammation, Barrier Function, and Therapeutic Potential. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research
items.summary: This review paper examines graphene oxide, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, as a versatile and sustainable platform with broad applicability across energy, environmental, and healthcare domains. The authors survey the synthesis chemistry and property modification strategies that give graphene oxide its distinctive characteristics, including tunable band gap, high surface area, structural flexibility, and adaptable surface chemistry spanning hydrophobic to hydrophilic configurations across zero- to three-dimensional nanostructures. The review covers a wide range of applications, encompassing environmental remediation such as adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of microplastics, pathogens, and toxic compounds, as well as energy technologies including carbon dioxide photoreduction, hydrogen production, and battery systems. Additional attention is given to sensing platforms, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering for ultrasensitive molecular detection and gas sensing, alongside various healthcare applications. The authors also identify persistent challenges facing graphene oxide, notably scalability limitations, long-term stability concerns, and reproducibility issues, and propose potential solutions to enable its sustainable real-world and industrial deployment.
items.citation: S.N. Thakur, Ayush Badoni, Rupam Sharma et al.. Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research. npj Materials Sustainability. 2026

items.title: Dietary polyphenols maintain human health through modulation of gut microbiota
items.summary: This review examines the bidirectional relationship between dietary polyphenols and the gut microbiota and its consequences for human health and disease. The authors surveyed how polyphenols, which are abundant in plant-derived foods and possess antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer properties, modulate the composition and function of the gut microbial community. In turn, the gut microbiota metabolizes polyphenols and governs their bioavailability, generating bioactive signalling metabolites that exert systemic physiological effects. Disruption of this relationship, termed dysbiosis, is linked to a range of gastrointestinal and systemic conditions including inflammatory bowel diseases, metabolic syndromes, and neurological disorders. The review concludes that fully characterizing this reciprocal polyphenol–microbiota interplay is essential for developing dietary or therapeutic interventions aimed at preserving host health and mitigating disease.
items.citation: Mithun Rudrapal, André M. de Oliveira, Ravi Pratap Singh. Dietary polyphenols maintain human health through modulation of gut microbiota. Frontiers in Pharmacology. 2026

items.title: Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards
items.summary: This paper investigates whether a unified large multimodal model capable of both visual understanding and image generation can improve itself using only unlabeled images, without relying on human annotations or external reward models. The authors propose a self-evolving training framework built around three internal roles: a Proposer that generates visual questions, a Solver that answers and evaluates them, and a Generator that synthesizes images, with training driven entirely by self-derived consistency signals. To stabilize training, they introduce a token-level prediction uncertainty measure called Solver Token Entropy as a continuous difficulty signal, and design a multi-scale internal evaluation scheme for image generation that couples question-answer fidelity with cycle-consistent captioning. The framework is architecture-agnostic, demonstrated across diffusion-based, rectified-flow, and autoregressive model families using only each backbone's native interface. The method consistently improves visual understanding across multiple benchmarks and also enhances image generation quality, demonstrating that unified multimodal models can self-improve both capabilities autonomously.
items.citation: Ritesh Thawkar, Shravan Venkatraman, Omkar Thawakar et al.. Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Scalable Behavior Cloning with Open Data, Training, and Evaluation
items.summary: This paper introduces an open-source framework for robotic manipulation using behavior cloning, centered on a large teleoperation dataset spanning thousands of hours across hundreds of diverse tasks. The authors also release hardware specifications, training infrastructure, a simulation pipeline, and simulation teleoperation data, along with a co-training recipe that aligns simulated and real-world evaluations to enable cheaper ablation studies. Using this framework, they systematically compare architectural choices for Diffusion Transformer and Vision-Language-Action models, grounding comparisons in real-world robot evaluations. The resulting policies demonstrate competence on dexterous manipulation tasks such as folding boxes and extracting credit cards from wallets. The overarching goal is to provide the research community with a reproducible, accessible toolkit that levels the playing field for behavior cloning research.
items.citation: Arthur Allshire, Himanshu Gaurav Singh, Ritvik Singh et al.. Scalable Behavior Cloning with Open Data, Training, and Evaluation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks
items.summary: This paper investigates the limitations of standard softmax-based multihead attention in vision transformers, arguing that conventional softmax produces noisy attention patterns that dilute relevant features and reduce model performance. The authors propose Denoising Attention (DnA), a method that introduces both a positive query to identify class-relevant image features and a negative query to capture closely associated but irrelevant features, then projects these interactions into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles to improve separation and discriminability. DnA is evaluated on image classification, video understanding, and video large language models using a vision transformer backbone, demonstrating consistent performance improvements across all tasks. Extensive empirical analyses are provided to justify the design choices, particularly the use of two interacting subspaces and the denoising mechanism.
items.citation: Ron Campos, Subhajit Maity, Xin Li et al.. DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Don't Settle at the Mode! Mitigating Diversity Collapse in Pretrained Flow Models via Feature Self-Guidance
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of diversity collapse in flow-based generative models, where generating multiple images from the same text or image prompt tends to produce outputs that cluster around a single mode rather than exploring the full range of plausible outputs. The authors propose a training-free, plug-and-play self-guidance mechanism that works by dispersing the internal features of the flow model during batch generation, without relying on any external reward models or incurring substantial computational overhead. To prevent these dispersed features from drifting off the data manifold, a manifold regularization step is introduced to project them back onto the manifold, maintaining alignment with the input conditioning. The method integrates directly into pretrained flow models as a drop-in module with minimal added inference cost. Experiments across multiple conditional generation tasks, including text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference image generation in both multi-step and few-step settings, demonstrate meaningful improvements in sample diversity without sacrificing output fidelity.
items.citation: Pradhaan S Bhat, Rishubh Parihar, Abhijnya Bhat et al.. Don't Settle at the Mode! Mitigating Diversity Collapse in Pretrained Flow Models via Feature Self-Guidance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dirac fermions in non-Hermitian magnetic fields: Zero modes and index theorem
items.summary: This paper investigates non-Hermitian (NH) extensions of Dirac theory in which a spatially modulated anti-Hermitian mass-like term acts as a NH gauge field, generating effective NH magnetic fields. The authors show analytically that when a planar Dirac system encloses a finite flux of such NH magnetic fields, a set of spatially localized zero-energy eigenmodes emerges that are purely right-handed or left-handed, extending the conventional Atiyah-Singer index theorem to the non-Hermitian setting. These zero modes and their associated flat bands are confirmed numerically through microscopic simulations on graphene's honeycomb lattice, providing a concrete lattice realization. The paper discusses potential experimental platforms for observing these effects and argues that the resulting NH zero-mode flat bands could serve as a foundation for phenomena such as NH magnetic catalysis, NH fractional topological phases, and NH chiral anomaly.
items.citation: Christopher A. Leong, Bitan Roy. Dirac fermions in non-Hermitian magnetic fields: Zero modes and index theorem. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs
items.summary: This paper investigates whether large language models can be improved through reinforcement learning on optimization tasks that lack ground-truth solutions, a setting where standard reward-based training approaches typically fail. The authors introduce a framework called RiVER that uses deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision and applies group-relative reinforcement learning with calibrated reward shaping to address two identified failure modes: scale dominance, where score magnitude differences across instances distort learning, and frequency dominance, where commonly sampled but suboptimal solutions overshadow rare high-quality ones. RiVER was trained on heuristic contest programming tasks and evaluated on several coding benchmarks spanning both score-based and exact-solution problem types. The framework meaningfully improved performance on the score-based benchmark but, crucially, also transferred to exact-solution benchmarks despite never training on tasks with ground-truth answers, while baselines using raw execution scores failed to achieve this transfer. These findings suggest that score-based optimization tasks with properly calibrated rewards can serve as effective training environments for developing general coding ability in the absence of ground-truth solutions.
items.citation: Yingyu Lin, Qiyue Gao, Nikki Lijing Kuang et al.. Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.
items.summary: The paper studied whether synthetic epitopes derived from Group B Streptococcus proteins could serve as the basis for a novel ELISA-based immunodiagnostic test to detect GBS carriage in pregnant women, offering an alternative to the current gold standard of bacterial culture, which is slow and can yield ambiguous results. The researchers identified three epitopes from the GBS proteins GroEL, enolase, and EF-Tu, produced them as synthetic peptides, and used them individually and in combination as ELISA antigens to detect IgG, IgM, and IgA antibodies in serum samples from GBS-positive and GBS-negative pregnant women. All three peptides, whether used alone or mixed, significantly distinguished between the two groups, with the enolase- and EF-Tu-derived epitopes performing best individually due to their high immunoreactivity at lower concentrations. Combining the epitopes in various mixtures further improved immunoreactivity beyond what any single peptide achieved. The authors conclude that this epitope-based ELISA represents a promising and potentially pioneering diagnostic prototype with no current equivalent on the market.
items.citation: Brzychczy-Włoch M, Dobrut A, Drożdż K et al.. Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.. Applied microbiology and biotechnology. 2026

items.title: Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a syndromic molecular panel for central nervous system infections by comparing it against conventional culture methods using cerebrospinal fluid samples collected over three years at a national reference center. The researchers assessed concordance, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values between the two approaches across a large dataset of paired results. The syndromic panel demonstrated high specificity and strong negative predictive value when restricted to its included bacterial targets, but overall sensitivity was substantially lower due to pathogens falling outside the panel's coverage. Conventional culture identified clinically relevant organisms not targeted by the panel, including certain staphylococcal and streptococcal species as well as rare environmental pathogens, underscoring its indispensable complementary role. The authors conclude that syndromic molecular panels should not be used as standalone diagnostics and that optimal management of central nervous system infections requires systematic integration of both approaches, alongside development of next-generation platforms with broader pathogen coverage.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Sberna G, D'Arezzo S, Valli MB et al.. Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the LIAISON PLEX Gram-Positive Blood Culture Assay, a multiplexed molecular test designed to rapidly identify gram-positive bacterial pathogens and antibiotic resistance markers directly from positive blood culture bottles. The assay targets thirteen gram-positive bacterial species and four antibiotic resistance markers, and its performance was assessed across prospective, pre-selected, and contrived blood culture samples in a multisite design. The assay demonstrated a very high valid rate along with excellent overall sensitivity and specificity across all targets, and it successfully detected the vast majority of inclusivity strains tested while showing minimal cross-reactivity with off-panel species. Performance was maintained across blood culture bottles from multiple manufacturers, in the presence of potential interfering substances, and in polymicrobial infection scenarios. The authors conclude that the assay offers fast, accurate, and reliable detection of gram-positive organisms and their resistance genes, with potential to support antibiotic stewardship and patient management decisions.
items.citation: Chow S-K, Stevig C, Granato P et al.. Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the real-world diagnostic performance and clinical utility of multiplex polymerase chain reaction panels designed to detect multiple pathogens simultaneously from cerebrospinal fluid in patients with suspected meningitis or encephalitis. Researchers retrospectively examined cerebrospinal fluid samples tested over a five-year period at a tertiary care hospital, reviewing test results alongside clinical data and subsequent management decisions. Viral pathogens dominated the positive detections, with human herpesvirus-6, enterovirus, and herpes simplex virus type-1 being most frequently identified, while Streptococcus pneumoniae was the most common bacterial finding. Despite the panels' capacity for rapid pathogen identification, fewer than half of positive results were associated with a documented change in diagnosis or treatment, and repeat testing after an initial negative result offered minimal additional diagnostic yield. The authors conclude that inappropriate test ordering and insufficient clinician familiarity with panel limitations substantially diminish the clinical impact of these tools, calling for clearer testing criteria and improved awareness to enhance diagnostic efficiency.
items.venue: The Indian journal of medical research
items.citation: Gürler M, Karaoğlu Ç, Yurtcu EE et al.. Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.. The Indian journal of medical research. 2026

items.title: Intrapartum Molecular Detection of Group B <i>Streptococcus</i>: Real-World Evaluation of Multiple Point-of-Care Platforms and the Potential Role of Lysis Efficiency.
items.summary: This study evaluated multiple rapid molecular point-of-care tests for detecting Group B Streptococcus colonisation during labour, addressing the limitation that antenatal screening does not reliably predict intrapartum status. Six molecular assays and one antigen-based test were prospectively assessed under real-world conditions using vaginal-rectal swabs collected at admission from pregnant women at term, with conventional intrapartum culture serving as the reference standard. Diagnostic performance varied considerably across platforms, with sensitivity ranging widely while specificity was generally more consistent, and several culture-positive cases were missed by all molecular methods tested. The authors observed that differences in cell lysis procedures between platforms may partly explain performance variability, though this could not be formally confirmed. The findings emphasise that substantial platform-to-platform variability exists under routine clinical conditions and that local validation is necessary before implementing any of these tests in practice.
items.citation: Serrari M, Bianchi L, Tré-Hardy M et al.. Intrapartum Molecular Detection of Group B <i>Streptococcus</i>: Real-World Evaluation of Multiple Point-of-Care Platforms and the Potential Role of Lysis Efficiency.. Microorganisms. 2026

query: point-of-care nucleic acid amplification test infectious disease

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing role in laboratory medicine and clinical diagnostics. The authors conducted a comprehensive survey of innovations in chip design and fabrication—including three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates—alongside progress in integrating advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms offering self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. These combined technologies have expanded applications across cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The review also identifies ongoing challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic platforms can be broadly translated into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies
items.summary: Severe community-acquired pneumonia remains a significant public health burden due to its frequency, complications, and treatment costs. The paper reviews current understanding and ongoing controversies in the management of this condition, including the challenge of selecting appropriate empirical antibiotic therapy in the context of resistant pathogens and the emerging but incompletely evaluated role of rapid molecular diagnostic techniques. Treatment duration is trending shorter and may be guided by clinical markers or biomarkers, while the potential for immunomodulatory therapy has gained attention given that the condition involves dysregulated systemic inflammation. Recent large randomized trials of corticosteroids have produced conflicting results, leaving unresolved which patient subgroups — potentially those with marked inflammatory responses but not those with influenza — might benefit, and some macrolides are also being explored for immunomodulatory properties. The review concludes that supportive care, especially tailored respiratory support, remains a cornerstone of management and must be individualized to disease severity.
items.citation: Pierre-François Dequin, A Torres, M. Bassetti et al.. Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies. Intensive Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.
items.summary: This paper developed and evaluated a novel electrochemical diagnostic platform that combines isothermal nucleic acid amplification with real-time voltammetric detection for identifying viral pathogens. The system detects amplification-associated proton release using redox probing of a pH-sensing compound, offering an alternative to optical detection methods that can impede conventional isothermal approaches like LAMP. The platform was tested against multiple respiratory viruses including SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV A using RNA purified from clinical samples, demonstrating high concordance with RT-qPCR as the reference standard. An extraction-free proof-of-concept workflow was also validated using equine nasal swab samples for a veterinary pathogen, with correct classification of all tested samples. The authors conclude that real-time voltammetric LAMP is feasible across diverse sample types and pathogens, though they acknowledge that broader point-of-care validation in additional matrices and prospective cohorts is still needed.
items.citation: Munita R, Lyttleton R, Danefur E et al.. A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for rapid, decentralized testing outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. They surveyed extraction strategies including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic approaches, emphasizing designs that remain simple, portable, and equipment-free. Amplification methods reviewed include both thermal cycling approaches such as PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all of which have been adapted for low-power and portable conditions. On the detection side, the review covers a spectrum from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, which offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.
items.summary: This study developed and evaluated a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a point-of-care testing instrument for detecting three bacterial pathogens — *Streptococcus pneumoniae*, *Streptococcus agalactiae*, and *Streptococcus pyogenes* — directly from cerebrospinal fluid. The approach was designed to automate the entire workflow from sample input to result output, eliminating the need for bacterial culture. The assay demonstrated very low detection limits and no cross-reactivity with other common pathogens, and it reduced total testing time compared to manual extraction paired with conventional real-time PCR. When evaluated on clinical cerebrospinal fluid and non-cerebrospinal fluid samples, the point-of-care method identified additional positive cases that conventional real-time PCR had missed. The authors conclude that this integrated system offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally streamlined approach to diagnosing bacterial meningitis-related pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Wu J, Zhao Z, Pang P et al.. A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.
items.summary: This review paper examined isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods as alternatives to conventional PCR-based diagnostics. The authors surveyed the principles, detection methods, and applications of the most widely used techniques, including loop-mediated isothermal amplification, helicase-dependent amplification, rolling circle amplification, strand displacement amplification, nucleic acid sequence-based amplification, and recombinase polymerase amplification. Unlike PCR, these methods operate at a constant temperature and do not require thermal cycling equipment, making them particularly suited for resource-limited settings that demand rapid, affordable, and simple nucleic acid detection. The review addresses a gap in the existing literature by comprehensively consolidating reaction mechanisms, product detection strategies, and clinical diagnostic applications in a single work. The authors conclude that isothermal amplification techniques offer significant promise for broadening access to molecular diagnostics across research and clinical practice, and they discuss future perspectives for the field.
items.citation: Amare Amdiyee A, Sisay Tessema T. Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.. Practical laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examines the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, motivated by the inadequacies of conventional culture-based methods and the growing threat of antimicrobial resistance. The authors conduct a structured comparative analysis of major platforms, including PCR and its variants, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, turnaround time, and multiplexing capability. The review also critically assesses how artificial intelligence and machine learning are being integrated into molecular diagnostic workflows to support antimicrobial resistance prediction and clinical decision-making. Additionally, the translational feasibility of these technologies for point-of-care deployment is considered, accounting for clinical validation requirements and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude by outlining an integrated perspective on current molecular strategies and identifying key challenges and future directions for advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.
items.summary: This study developed a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a portable, fully automated point-of-care testing platform for detecting three key meningitis pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid. The assay performs nested amplification within a closed system using conventional primers and probes, reducing contamination risk and operational complexity, and was evaluated both analytically and clinically using simulated and real cerebrospinal fluid samples. The method demonstrated very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity against a broad panel of non-target microorganisms, and delivered results in under two hours. Clinical testing against conventional quantitative PCR revealed near-perfect agreement, with the new assay additionally detecting more cases of one fungal pathogen, suggesting enhanced sensitivity under real-world conditions. The authors conclude that the platform offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally straightforward approach for infectious meningitis diagnostics, though they note that validation in larger patient cohorts is needed.
items.citation: Zhang D, Wang J, Zhao Z et al.. A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey
items.summary: This survey examines the intersection of foundation models and computational healthcare through a data-centric lens, focusing on how data quality, scale, and characterization drive advances in clinical AI. The authors review a broad range of data-centric approaches spanning model pre-training through inference, addressing longstanding challenges in healthcare such as data scarcity, annotation burden, patient privacy, and ethical considerations. The survey also explores AI security, model assessment, and alignment with human values as critical dimensions of deploying foundation models in clinical settings. The authors conclude with an outlook on how foundation model-based analytics can improve patient outcomes and clinical workflows as healthcare AI continues to evolve.
items.citation: Yunkun Zhang, Jin Gao, Zheling Tan et al.. Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey. ACM Computing Surveys. 2026

items.title: Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions
items.citation: Lorenzo Galluzzi, Oliver Kepp, Laurence Zitvogel et al.. Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions. Cancer Cell. 2025

items.title: Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
items.summary: This review paper examines acute pancreatitis, a severe inflammatory condition whose global incidence is rising with notable regional variation in both frequency and etiology, including gallstone disease, hypertriglyceridemia, and alcohol abuse. The authors synthesize current knowledge on disease definition and classification, as well as advances in clinical scoring systems, biomarkers, and artificial intelligence-based predictive models intended to support early risk stratification and clinical decision-making. The review also details the multilevel pathophysiological mechanisms underlying the condition, encompassing aberrant enzymatic activation, calcium overload, impaired autophagy, inflammatory cascades, and various modes of pancreatic acinar cell death. From a therapeutic standpoint, the paper covers early- and late-stage management strategies, adjunctive approaches drawn from traditional Chinese medicine, and individualized treatment considerations for special patient populations. Emerging interventions such as nanotechnology and extracellular vesicle-based therapies are additionally highlighted as promising future directions for research and clinical innovation.
items.citation: Qian Hu, Yue Hu, Chunlu Tan et al.. Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation
items.summary: This review examines the current landscape of biosensing technologies designed to detect and analyze extracellular vesicles, which carry molecular cargo that reflects the biological state of their parent cells and thus hold strong potential as noninvasive diagnostic biomarkers. The authors survey a range of detection modalities—including surface plasmon resonance, electrochemical, fluorescence, and magnetic systems—evaluating how each has advanced sensitivity and specificity for EV analysis. The review also addresses efforts to standardize EV research through established guidelines and analytical validation frameworks, and explores how multiplexed profiling at the single-vesicle level combined with machine learning can improve diagnostic accuracy and account for biological heterogeneity. Clinical applications across cancer, neurological, cardiometabolic, and infectious diseases are discussed to illustrate translational progress. The authors conclude by identifying outstanding challenges in manufacturing, quality control, and point-of-care implementation, and propose a unified framework intended to integrate EV biosensing into precision diagnostics and personalized medicine.
items.citation: Seong-Jun Choi, Jaewon Choi, Jin Kim et al.. Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation. Molecules. 2026

items.title: Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation
items.summary: This qualitative study explored perceptions of integrating a point-of-care triage test measuring interferon-gamma-induced protein 10 (IP-10), a chemokine correlated with HIV viral load, into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique. Researchers conducted multiple rounds of interviews with people living with HIV undergoing IP-10 testing, healthcare providers, and HIV programme stakeholders across different health system levels, nested within a clinical evaluation at two primary healthcare facilities. Participants widely valued viral load monitoring but described significant barriers to access, including laboratory delays and health system constraints. The IP-10 triage test was broadly acceptable, with same-day results perceived to reduce patient anxiety, support treatment adherence, and enable timely clinical decision-making by identifying those needing confirmatory viral load testing. However, concerns about test accuracy and the continued necessity of confirmatory testing were raised, with successful implementation deemed dependent on adequate training, workflow integration, quality assurance, and clear patient communication.
items.citation: Myburgh H, Saura-Lázaro A, van den Bogaart E et al.. Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation. 2026

items.title: Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.
items.summary: This qualitative study investigated the implementation bottlenecks of near point-of-care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people living with HIV in Tanzania, from the perspective of healthcare workers. The researchers conducted clinic observations and in-depth interviews with healthcare workers at multiple time points across Tanzanian intervention sites participating in a larger cluster-randomised controlled trial, using a thematic framework analysis guided by the Measurement Instrument for Determinants of Innovations. The findings revealed that near point-of-care testing improved result turnaround time and enabled same-day counselling, which motivated both healthcare workers and patients, and was further supported by compatibility with existing clinical practices, teamwork, and management commitment. However, significant barriers were identified, including patients waiting extended periods for results, scarcity of physical resources such as rooms and electricity, staff shortages, and delays caused by sample transport to nearby laboratories. The authors conclude that realising the full potential of near point-of-care viral load monitoring requires targeted investment in staff training, infrastructure, sample handling procedures, and alignment with clinical guidelines.
items.citation: Msoka P, Mtenga A, Maro R et al.. Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.
items.summary: This study examined viral suppression, treatment adherence, drug exposure, and mental health among peripartum adolescent girls and young women living with HIV in Zimbabwe. Participants aged fifteen to twenty-four years who were in the peripartum period and on tenofovir-based antiretroviral therapy were enrolled into a randomized controlled and observational cohort study, with point-of-care viral load testing and urine tenofovir detection used alongside validated mental health screening tools. The researchers found high rates of viral suppression at baseline, with the large majority of participants achieving undetectable viral loads, and suppression was strongly associated with detectable tenofovir in urine and higher self-reported adherence. Socioeconomic factors including food security, financial security, and marital status were also significantly associated with viral suppression. Mental health outcomes were generally positive, with most participants showing minimal depression and anxiety, though moderate HIV-related stigma was common among over half the cohort.
items.venue: Journal of the International AIDS Society
items.citation: Chawana-Mutingwende TD, Ngara B, Mudadi LS et al.. Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.. Journal of the International AIDS Society. 2026

items.title: Feasibility and reliability of decentralized HIV-1 viral load monitoring on self-sampled blood.
items.summary: This study evaluated whether home-based blood self-sampling using a capillary collection device could reliably support HIV-1 viral load monitoring as an alternative to clinic-based testing. Participants recruited at an outpatient clinic collected their own blood samples at home, which were then tested for HIV RNA and compared against conventional plasma samples from the same individuals, with additional stored low-level viremic samples also assessed. While overall correlation between self-sampled and conventionally sampled results was acceptable, a substantial proportion of self-collected samples had insufficient plasma volume for analysis, and low-level viremia was missed in several cases where conventional testing detected detectable virus. User-friendliness was rated positively by only around half of participants, and overall return rates were modest. The authors conclude that meaningful technical and procedural improvements are needed before this approach can be considered reliable for routine clinical use.
items.citation: Mendes de Leon KF, Jurriaans S, Klungers RD et al.. Feasibility and reliability of decentralized HIV-1 viral load monitoring on self-sampled blood.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Herpes Simplex Virus Type 2 Screening in Persons with and Without HIV: Evidence, Challenges, and Future Directions
items.summary: This review examined the current evidence and guidelines surrounding serologic screening for herpes simplex virus type 2, particularly in the context of HIV prevention and care. The authors synthesized existing guidance, test performance data, available therapeutics, and psychosocial considerations to evaluate the role of screening across different populations. International guidelines recommend against routine population-level serologic screening in asymptomatic individuals, citing poor test specificity, lack of curative or highly potent antiviral options, absence of demonstrated population-level benefit, and potential psychosocial harms from diagnosis. Instead, evidence supports targeted screening in defined clinical contexts, such as among people living with HIV, sex partners of infected individuals, certain pregnant women, and those with recurrent or atypical symptoms, where results can directly guide management. The authors conclude that advances in diagnostics, novel antivirals, and therapeutic or curative vaccines may eventually shift the risk-benefit balance to justify broader routine screening, but such a shift requires first demonstrating measurable reductions in disease burden or transmission at the population level.
items.citation: Laith J. Abu-Raddad, Hiam Chemaitelly, Anna Wald et al.. Herpes Simplex Virus Type 2 Screening in Persons with and Without HIV: Evidence, Challenges, and Future Directions. Current HIV/AIDS Reports. 2026

items.title: Correlates of Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) Positivity and Implementation of Point-of-Care STI Testing at a Mobile Clinic for Women Who Inject Drugs in Seattle, WA.
items.summary: This study examined the prevalence of sexually transmitted infections among women who inject drugs and tested whether point-of-care STI testing could be feasibly and acceptably implemented at a mobile clinic serving this population in Seattle. Researchers enrolled women with a history of injecting drugs and offered point-of-care testing for multiple STIs at baseline and follow-up visits, alongside surveys assessing participant and provider experiences. They found notably high rates of trichomonas positivity, with STI positivity overall associated with having multiple sexual partners and engaging in transactional sex. Same-day treatment was successfully delivered in the vast majority of positive cases, and most participants preferred point-of-care testing over traditional methods, though providers reported logistical challenges and difficulties navigating competing participant priorities. The authors conclude that low-barrier, mobile point-of-care STI testing is both acceptable and feasible for this population and facilitates timely treatment.
items.citation: Fessler AG, Corcorran MA, Violette LR et al.. Correlates of Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) Positivity and Implementation of Point-of-Care STI Testing at a Mobile Clinic for Women Who Inject Drugs in Seattle, WA.. Sexually transmitted diseases. 2026

items.title: Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.
items.summary: This study examined the implementation of rapid point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in non-clinical, community outreach settings in an urban environment. Researchers conducted a program evaluation and retrospective data review of individuals tested at these sites, using statistical tests to explore associations between positive results and demographic characteristics or symptom presence. The testing platform delivered results within thirty minutes, and all individuals who tested positive for either infection were referred for treatment within one day of diagnosis. No statistically significant associations were found between test positivity and demographic factors or symptoms, though the small sample size limited these analyses. The authors conclude that point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing can facilitate rapid diagnosis and timely treatment referral in outreach settings, while calling for further research into scalability, cost, and treatment completion rates.
items.citation: Blanchard HL, Gonzalez E, Chow J. Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.. Sexual health. 2026

items.title: From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.
items.summary: This paper examined the challenges and opportunities involved in transitioning from syndromic management to diagnostic-led management of sexually transmitted infections in low-resource settings, with a focus on sub-Saharan Africa. An expert meeting brought together specialists in diagnostics, implementation research, policy, regulatory affairs, and clinical practice to identify priorities and strategies for introducing and scaling up point-of-care STI testing. The group analyzed barriers and enablers at multiple levels, ranging from individual patient uptake to health service delivery, national policy, and global regulatory frameworks. Recommendations spanned improving patient knowledge about STIs, optimizing clinical workflows, enabling testing outside traditional facilities, updating treatment guidelines, strengthening laboratory quality assurance, harmonizing regulatory requirements across regions, and expanding manufacturing capacity. The authors conclude that coordinated action across all these levels is necessary to achieve equitable access to quality STI diagnostics, which they argue will reduce overtreatment, undertreatment, antimicrobial resistance, and improve overall STI control.
items.citation: Manguro GO, Ray J, Schocken C et al.. From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.
items.summary: This study examined the prospective acceptability of the genital inflammation test (GIFT), a point-of-care screening tool designed to detect genital inflammation associated with sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in asymptomatic women.
