Researchers conducted in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with women, healthcare professionals, and decision-makers across South Africa and Zimbabwe, using a theoretical framework of acceptability to guide thematic analysis. Women expressed strong enthusiasm for sexual health diagnostics generally, but their acceptance of GIFT specifically declined once they understood it functions as a preliminary screen requiring follow-up confirmatory testing, given the cost and scarcity of such tests in these settings. Healthcare professionals and decision-makers similarly questioned the practical utility of GIFT within resource-constrained health systems, while also acknowledging its potential to catalyze the development of cheaper, infrastructure-light diagnostics. The findings highlight that acceptability of novel biomedical tools is fundamentally shaped by health system context, pointing to the need for affordable follow-on diagnostics, clear communication strategies, and co-designed care pathways.
items.citation: Karumazondo J, Bernays S, Naidoo J et al.. Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.. Global public health. 2026

items.title: Implementing STI point-of-care testing using a whole-system programme model: the ProSPiRO implementation research protocol.
items.summary: This protocol paper describes a planned implementation research study examining how to integrate sexually transmitted infection point-of-care testing into diverse health system settings. The researchers developed a whole-system programme model delivered through stakeholder-led workshops that can be locally adapted across multiple countries, with participating sites spanning sexual and reproductive health services, antenatal care, and general practice. Sites will implement point-of-care tests for selected pathogens including chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HIV, and syphilis over one year, with evaluation using a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative clinical outcomes, economic analysis, and qualitative data from workshops, focus groups, and interviews. The study aims to assess acceptability, feasibility, and suitability of the programme model, with primary outcomes focused on same-day diagnosis and treatment, service capacity, and cost consequences relative to usual care. The protocol is designed to identify which components of the model work, for whom, and under what circumstances, with the goal of generating policy-relevant evidence to support equitable and sustainable scale-up of point-of-care testing.
items.citation: Fuller SS, Padovese V, Sadiq ST et al.. Implementing STI point-of-care testing using a whole-system programme model: the ProSPiRO implementation research protocol.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.
items.summary: This scoping review systematically mapped research on artificial intelligence-enhanced point-of-care and rapid diagnostic technologies for infectious diseases in resource-limited settings, focusing on disease coverage, geographic representation, and validation quality. Following established scoping review methodology, the authors searched five major databases for studies published over approximately a decade and included over two hundred studies after screening. The field was found to be growing rapidly, with nearly half of included studies published in the most recent period, and dominated by work on COVID-19, malaria, and tuberculosis, while neglected tropical diseases were substantially underrepresented. Convolutional neural networks were the most commonly employed artificial intelligence approach, applied across modalities including microscopy, molecular diagnostics, biosensors, and rapid diagnostic tests. Despite the growth of the field, the review identified critical gaps in validation rigor—with only a small fraction of studies reporting prospective field validation—as well as geographic concentration in East Africa and South Asia that leaves West Africa and Latin America underrepresented, underscoring the need for broader disease coverage, rigorous field validation, and greater involvement of end-user communities.
items.venue: Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH
items.citation: Farquhar H. AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.. Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH. 2026

items.title: Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine
items.citation: Zelinskyy G, Koval T, Schrammel U et al.. Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Advancing diagnostics for Chagas disease: key product characteristics and harmonized evaluation strategies - an expert meeting report.
items.summary: This paper addresses the critical diagnostic gap in Chagas disease, a parasitic illness that disproportionately affects underserved Latin American populations and remains severely underdiagnosed and undertreated. An expert group comprising researchers, test developers, and health authorities convened to build consensus around improving diagnostic tools and their evaluation. The group focused on three key areas: developing a harmonized protocol for evaluating rapid diagnostic tests, defining preferred characteristics for molecular diagnostics, and identifying the economic evidence needed to drive meaningful change in primary healthcare settings. Their outputs include practical recommendations intended to guide test development and evaluation, while also addressing how to stimulate investment in this neglected area. The paper ultimately proposes a path forward to close the diagnostic gap and support the goal of disease elimination by the end of the decade.
items.citation: Bohorquez LC, Schijman AG, Perez F et al.. Advancing diagnostics for Chagas disease: key product characteristics and harmonized evaluation strategies - an expert meeting report.. BMC infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: The impact of interventions to modify antibiotic use in children with suspected infections in ambulatory healthcare settings in LMICs: a systematic review.
items.summary: This systematic review examined interventions designed to optimize antibiotic use in children with suspected infections attending ambulatory healthcare settings in low- and middle-income countries, where antimicrobial resistance poses a growing threat to child health. The authors searched multiple major databases and regional literature sources, ultimately including studies that met rigorous quality criteria, the majority of which were randomized trials conducted across a range of low- and middle-income country settings. The review found that electronic decision-support algorithms and enhanced point-of-care tests showed promise in reducing antibiotic prescriptions in some settings, such as Tanzania, Ghana, and Burkina Faso, though results were inconsistent across countries including Uganda, Myanmar, and Thailand. Encouragingly, most studies demonstrated that reductions in prescribing did not compromise clinical outcomes, suggesting these approaches can be deployed without apparent harm to patients. The authors conclude that combined strategies integrating algorithmic decision support, rapid diagnostics, and provider education hold promise, but call for future research that evaluates prescription appropriateness and microbiological outcomes, and that accounts for the implementation feasibility across diverse contexts.
items.citation: Hardman JS, Chareka G, O'Mahony E et al.. The impact of interventions to modify antibiotic use in children with suspected infections in ambulatory healthcare settings in LMICs: a systematic review.. JAC-antimicrobial resistance. 2026

items.title: Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.
items.summary: This study examined the prospective acceptability of a point-of-care genital inflammation screening test, known as GIFT, designed to identify asymptomatic women who may have sexually transmitted infections or bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa. The researchers conducted in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with women, healthcare professionals, and decision-makers, analyzing findings through an established framework of acceptability. Women initially expressed strong interest in tools to support their sexual health and viewed diagnostic testing positively, but their enthusiasm diminished when they understood that a positive GIFT result would require follow-up confirmatory testing, given concerns about the cost and limited availability of such tests. Healthcare professionals and decision-makers similarly questioned the practical utility of GIFT within resource-constrained health systems, while acknowledging its potential to catalyze the development of cheaper, infrastructure-light diagnostics. The findings highlight that the acceptability of novel biomedical technologies is substantially shaped by health system context, emphasizing the importance of affordable care pathways, clear communication, and co-design with end users.
items.citation: Karumazondo J, Bernays S, Naidoo J et al.. Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.. Global public health. 2026
